rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Snooker
rules.label: Manual of Style/Snooker
sections: Recording match results and frame scores
key_statements: More information on how the infobox should be used can be found in the template documentation.
Per the Manual of Style, on dashes, do not use hyphens between scores and date ranges; instead, use en-dashes: .
In prose sections, to avoid potentially awkward line breaks, use  around the entire score construction, or use a non-breaking en-dash: .
Scores should never be added or updated while a session is in progress, including during a mid-session interval.
To avoid edit warring between established editors and newcomers, it is still important to assume good faith and comply with the three-revert rule when removing live scores.

== Neutral tone in biography articles ==

Take care to avoid the use of weasel-wording, peacock language and wikipuffery in describing individuals and their accomplishments/failures.
Do not write Wikipedia articles in such a vein, as all of these tactics are violations of Wikipedia's neutral point-of-view policy, and please fix problems like these when you encounter them.
In order for any such phrasing to appear in an article, it must be a properly cited direct quotation, from a reliable source.
These tournaments shouldn't be abbreviated simply to "Masters", because the term denotes a specific and completely different tournament.
dos: More information on how the infobox should be used can be found in the template documentation.

(See also Manual of Style section about overbroad use of flags with politicized connotations.)

== References and external links ==

For the "External links" section in snooker articles:

#If something is a textual source upon which anything in the article is being drawn, then this should be a reference.
In order for any such phrasing to appear in an article, it must be a properly cited direct quotation, from a reliable source.

===Champion, championship, championships===
Championship is always singular when speaking of a specific event, and always plural when writing of a series or multiplicity of events:
* "Joe Bloggs won the 2009 Isle of Man Championship." (single event)
* "Joe Bloggs has won three Isle of Man Championships." (multiple events)
* "Joe Bloggs is a frequent competitor at the Isle of Man Championships." (series of events; if you wrote "frequent competitor at the Isle of Man Championship" this would rather crazily imply that this year he competed in the championship several times!)

"Championship" is only capitalised when used as part of the official name (or common short or extended version) of an event, e.g.
These tournaments shouldn't be abbreviated simply to "Masters", because the term denotes a specific and completely different tournament.
donts: Per the Manual of Style, on dashes, do not use hyphens between scores and date ranges; instead, use en-dashes: .
In prose sections, to avoid potentially awkward line breaks, use  around the entire score construction, or use a non-breaking en-dash: .
Scores should never be added or updated while a session is in progress, including during a mid-session interval.
To avoid edit warring between established editors and newcomers, it is still important to assume good faith and comply with the three-revert rule when removing live scores.

== Neutral tone in biography articles ==

Take care to avoid the use of weasel-wording, peacock language and wikipuffery in describing individuals and their accomplishments/failures.
Do not write Wikipedia articles in such a vein, as all of these tactics are violations of Wikipedia's neutral point-of-view policy, and please fix problems like these when you encounter them.

===Masters=== 
Masters in this context is always "Masters":
* "Joe Bloggs won the 2009 Isle of Man Masters." (single event)
* "Joe Bloggs has won three Isle of Man Masters." (multiple events; "Masters titles" would be better here, though, for clarity)
* "Joe Bloggs is a frequent competitor at the Isle of Man Masters." (series of events)
"Masters" is basically always capitalised because it is never really used outside of an actual event name (e.g.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers/Date autoformatting
rules.label: Manual of Style/Dates and numbers/Date autoformatting
key_statements: All links from articles should be of high value to the reader, that is, following them should genuinely help the reader understand the topic more fully.

<!--Dates before 1583 should not be wikilinked, lest they be autoformatted.|This is advice, not a statement of disadvantages--> Autoformatting into and out of this format could be misleading or erroneous for dates on the Julian Calendar, if one thought this format was truly ISO 8601.
One could make them links by using the standard piped-link notation, but the same advice applies here: such links should not be created unless they are of high value to the reader.
Date elements on disambiguation pages and section headings should never be linked; the same applies to quotations, unless the original quote is linked.
dos: All links from articles should be of high value to the reader, that is, following them should genuinely help the reader understand the topic more fully.
donts: <!--Dates before 1583 should not be wikilinked, lest they be autoformatted.|This is advice, not a statement of disadvantages--> Autoformatting into and out of this format could be misleading or erroneous for dates on the Julian Calendar, if one thought this format was truly ISO 8601.

(settings on first row) | rowspan="2" | What others will see [[#endnote_A|[A]]] |- | January 15, 2001 | 15 January 2001 | 2001 January 15 | 2001-01-15 | No preference |- | [[May 15 | 15 May | [[May 15 | [[May 15 |- | [[May 15 | 15 May | 15 May | 15 May | 15 May | 15 May |- | [[May 15, [[May 15|15 May [[2005 [[2005-05-15 | [[May 15, - | [[15 May [[May 15, [[May 15|15 May [[2005 [[2005-05-15 | 15 May [[May 15|15 May - |   [[2005-05-15 [[#endnote_B|[B]]] | [[May 15|15 May [[2005 [[2005-05-15 | 05-15 | 05-15 |- | align="left" colspan="8" |
  1. ^ Non-registered users and registered users not logged in
  2. ^ Editors are discouraged from using this format since non-registered users, who constitute the majority of readers, may see a hard-to-read date format.
|}
Even when surrounded by square brackets, those dates that do not contain both a day and a month—such as solitary months, solitary days of the week, solitary years, decades, centuries, and month-year combinations—are not autoformatted.
One could make them links by using the standard piped-link notation, but the same advice applies here: such links should not be created unless they are of high value to the reader.
Date elements on disambiguation pages and section headings should never be linked; the same applies to quotations, unless the original quote is linked.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Singapore-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Singapore-related articles
key_statements:
==Naming conventions==
===People===
In general, a biographical article should be named in the way the name of the person who is the article's subject usually appears in English-language publications in Singapore.

====Singaporeans with Chinese names====
Article titles should follow Wikipedia:Naming conventions (Chinese).
Where a specific romanisation system is used, conventions used by that system should be adhered to.
Hence, Pinyin names should have no spaces in the first name (Huang Wenyong, and not Huang Wen Yong), and diacritics should not be used in the article name.
Where a Chinese person usually indicates their surname at the beginning of the name, the use of the hatnote  is recommended.

====Singaporeans with Indian names====
Article titles should follow the manner in which the subjects of the articles normally appear in English-language publications in Singapore, e.g., "Balaji Sadasivan", not "Sadasivan Balaji".
When persons do not usually use the abbreviations s/o ("son of") or d/o ("daughter of") in their names, these words should not be added (e.g., "Balaji Sadasivan", not "Balaji s/o Sadasivan").
Likewise, where persons usually have these words in their names, the words should not be omitted.
Use of the hatnote  is recommended.
When persons do not usually use the words bin (Malay for "son of") or binte ("daughter of") in their names, these words should not be added (e.g., "Zubir Said", not "Zubir bin Said").
Likewise, where persons usually have these words in their names, the words should not be omitted.
Use of the hatnote  is recommended.
For example, "Hougang", which is used officially in English, should be used in preference to "Aukang" even though the latter is often used locally.

Use the template as follows:
*Alternative, non-English names should usually be listed once.
If the article has an infobox, the names should be listed there.
If not, the names should go in the first sentence of the lead.

*If the names have a complex history or vary considerably from language to language (e.g., Tekka Centre and Pedra Branca, Singapore), this should be discussed in a separate section and not in the lead.

===Measurements===
Measurements should be set out using the Imperial system in parentheses after the metric measurement.

:* Regardless of divisions, for a new single-member constituency (SMC) / GRC, the predecessor should be set to Constituency established, and if it is abolished, the successor should be Constituency abolished.

:* For parliamentary terms in a GRC, predecessor and successor should be set to "Party held ", where Party is/are the name(s) of the incumbent party/ies before the politician began representing the GRC and after they ceased to represent it.
dos:
==Naming conventions==
===People===
In general, a biographical article should be named in the way the name of the person who is the article's subject usually appears in English-language publications in Singapore.

====Singaporeans with Chinese names====
Article titles should follow Wikipedia:Naming conventions (Chinese).

In particular, this means that names more familiar to most English readers, and which are normally used in English-language publications in Singapore, should be used with no specific preference for any Chinese dialect or romanisation system.
Where a specific romanisation system is used, conventions used by that system should be adhered to.
Where a Chinese person usually indicates their surname at the beginning of the name, the use of the hatnote  is recommended.

Where it is necessary to disambiguate the article as a result, either the full namee.g., opposition politician) vs Tony Tan (Keng Yam) (former president)or a disambiguating term in parenthesese.g., Christopher Lee (Singaporean actor)should be used: see Wikipedia:Disambiguation and Wikipedia:Naming conventions (people).

====Singaporeans with Indian names====
Article titles should follow the manner in which the subjects of the articles normally appear in English-language publications in Singapore, e.g., "Balaji Sadasivan", not "Sadasivan Balaji".
Use of the hatnote  is recommended.
For example, "Hougang", which is used officially in English, should be used in preference to "Aukang" even though the latter is often used locally.

"P<u>u</u>nggol" should be spelt as indicated when referring to the town, but as "P<u>o</u>nggol" in the road name "Ponggol Seventeenth Avenue" as that is the official name of the road in English; compare also Ch<u>o</u>a Chu Kang MRT/LRT station and Ch<u>u</u>a Chu Kang Group Representation Constituency.

Therefore, in accordance with Wikipedia:Manual of Style (dates and numbers)#Strong national ties to a topic, in articles:
*dates should be set out with the day before the month; and
*the parameter "df=yes" ("df" standing for "day first") should be used with the templates , , , , and related templates.

===Lead section===
*Where appropriate, in the lead section of Singapore-related articles (such as articles about Singaporean festivals), the article title in English in boldface should be followed by translations of the title in parentheses in the three other official languages, ordered alphabetically as follows: Malay, Mandarin and Tamil.<ref>See the , Art.

Use the template as follows:
*Alternative, non-English names should usually be listed once.
If the article has an infobox, the names should be listed there.
If not, the names should go in the first sentence of the lead.

Likewise, where persons usually have these words in their names, the words should not be omitted.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers/Date Linking RFC
rules.label: Manual of Style/Dates and numbers/Date Linking RFC
sections: Is some method of date autoformatting desirable?
Support some method of date autoformatting
Oppose any method of date autoformatting
When to link to Month-Day articles?
Month-Day links should always be made
Month-Day links should be made in certain cases
Month-Day links should never be made
What about articles for one specific date in one specific year?
Year links should always be made

How to use "Year in Field" links
key_statements: This RFC seeks input about how editors and readers of Wikipedia expect dates within articles to be handled, including if they should be autoformatted and when dates should be linked.
Links should only be used where they help a reader understand an article.
However, I should point out that deprecated does not mean "removed as fast as possible" as some people seem to think it does.
I strongly want autoformating of dates that works but this should be independent of links.
In addition, the autoformatting can obscure problems that editors do not see, but the vast majority of our readers do see.

Karanacs (talk) 16:28, 26 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support as date links do not link to pages that are generally relevant to the context of the article they are in.
Links should only be made on meaningful relationships.
Using it for DA was a mistake and should be corrected asap.

--RexxS (talk) 03:44, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
:Just to be clear, since the title of this section has changed since I originally !voted: My comment was in support of the principle, "Dates should not be linked purely for the purpose of autoformatting".
My understanding of "should not" is that such links should be removed, not merely deprecated.

--RexxS (talk) 01:27, 1 December 2008 (UTC)
*Support linking should only be done for reasons of relevance.
Links in articles should always be able to take you to somewhere useful when you click on them.
If I click on a year link I will just about never get information that sheds light on the article.
The problem of showing dates in a way that fits a user's expectations is trivial in comparison, and should be handled by a different mechanism than links.

Anaxial (talk) 19:27, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support - I've never used autoformatting and I find it useless.
Date formatting should be determined the same as variations of English in articles.

Poltair (talk) 22:29, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support. I don't see why dates should be clickable at all.
i don't see why dates should be linked at all.

- SWTPC6800 (talk) 02:43, 29 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support I do not think it is a benefit to readers, and creates countless links that few actually use.
I do not see where these links are even a benefit to the article or readers at hand.
dos: This RFC seeks input about how editors and readers of Wikipedia expect dates within articles to be handled, including if they should be autoformatted and when dates should be linked.
Links should only be used where they help a reader understand an article.
However, I should point out that deprecated does not mean "removed as fast as possible" as some people seem to think it does.
I strongly want autoformating of dates that works but this should be independent of links.

I also believe UK centric articles should in the raw be "dd mmm yyyy" whilst US centric ones should probably be "mmm dd, yyyy" -- SGBailey (talk) 22:50, 25 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support - but only if there is an alternative method of date autoformatting available.
Links should only be made on meaningful relationships.
Using it for DA was a mistake and should be corrected asap.

--RexxS (talk) 01:27, 1 December 2008 (UTC)
*Support linking should only be done for reasons of relevance.

Yngvarr (t) (c) 14:57, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support Per all above, it's useless overlinking.--HereToHelp <sup>(talk to me)</sup> 15:27, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support I have always thought it was unnecessary, I completely agree with HereToHelp; Useless Overlinking <font face="Batik Regular">Highfields (talk, contribs, review)</font> 15:36, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support Is unnecessary, produces a "blue sea" effect and allows editors to be lazy with consistent formatting at the expense of our readers (it's sadly a far less hypothetical or theoretical problem than one might imagine).
Links in articles should always be able to take you to somewhere useful when you click on them.
The problem of showing dates in a way that fits a user's expectations is trivial in comparison, and should be handled by a different mechanism than links.
Date formatting should be determined the same as variations of English in articles.

Poltair (talk) 22:29, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support. I don't see why dates should be clickable at all.
i don't see why dates should be linked at all.

kilbad (talk) 03:47, 29 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support There should be some alternative for formatting, such as a template for dates -BarkerJr (talk) 04:15, 29 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support The linking doesn't really help the average reader, and it has marginal costs for editors.
donts: ) >= ()|<div style="border: 1px solid #501010; background-color:#f0d0d0; padding: 1em;"><div style="text-align: center;">This Request for Comment is now closed. Please do not modify the archive below and instead participate in discussions at WT:MOSNUM.

Sssoul (talk) 11:00, 25 November 2008 (UTC)

*Support - way overused and undermines the purpose of linking in Wikipedia.

HWV 258  21:36, 25 November 2008 (UTC) *Support - I see no useful purpose in autoformatting dates when the vast majority of our readers do not see the autoformatting, nor do I see the merit in linking to a random list of things that happened on a date.
In addition, the autoformatting can obscure problems that editors do not see, but the vast majority of our readers do see.

Karanacs (talk) 16:28, 26 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support as date links do not link to pages that are generally relevant to the context of the article they are in.

--RexxS (talk) 03:44, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
:Just to be clear, since the title of this section has changed since I originally !voted: My comment was in support of the principle, "Dates should not be linked purely for the purpose of autoformatting".
My understanding of "should not" is that such links should be removed, not merely deprecated.
If I click on a year link I will just about never get information that sheds light on the article.

Anaxial (talk) 19:27, 28 November 2008 (UTC)
*Support - I've never used autoformatting and I find it useless.

—Scheinwerfermann T·C21:04, 28 November 2008 (UTC) *Support Dates lead to random trivia lists that do not help improve the article, especially in articles on a less notable topic where the article linked to doesn't even mention it (I.E.
Dates should not be linked.

- SWTPC6800 (talk) 02:43, 29 November 2008 (UTC)

I do not see where these links are even a benefit to the article or readers at hand.

Sam Blacketer (talk) 21:45, 29 November 2008 (UTC)

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Lists
rules.label: Manual of Style/Lists
sections: Related topics (navigational lists)
List layout
Use an unordered list by default
Format list items consistently
Adding individual items to a list
key_statements: The items on these lists might include links to specific articles or other information, and must be supported with references like any article.
Embedded lists should be used only when appropriate; sometimes the information in a list is better presented as prose.
The content of a list is governed by the same content policies as prose, including principles of due weight and avoiding original research.

Ensure that list items have the same importance to the subject as would be required for the item to be included in the text of the article, according to Wikipedia policies and guidelines (including WP:Trivia sections).
For example, list of Indian language poets should link to both list of Indians and list of poets.
There is some controversy over how many links to articles and links to lists that should be put in any article.
Some feel the optimum number of links to lists that should be included at the end of any given article is zero, one, or two.
In general, when deciding what list to include, the same criteria used to decide what articles to include in the "See also" section should be used.
Editors should try to put themselves in the readers' frame of mind and ask "Where will I likely want to go after reading this article?".
As a general rule, the "See also" section should not repeat links that appear in the article's body.
*Dynamic list – a dynamic list is any list that may never be fully complete (since it will only include notable examples from a given category), or may require constant updates to remain current.

If users have some general idea of what they are looking for but do not know the specific terminology, they could browse the lists of basic topics and more comprehensive lists of topics, which in turn lead to most if not all of Wikipedia's lists, which in turn lead to related articles.
The list title should not be misleading and should normally not include abbreviations.

Additionally, an overly precise list title can be less useful and can make the list difficult to find; the precise inclusion criteria for the list should be spelled out in the lead section (see below), not the title.
Note that the word "famous" is considered an unnecessary  "peacock" embellishment and should not be used.
To suggest that a list in an article or section should be sorted, use .
Prose is preferred in articles because it allows the presentation of detail and clarification of context in a way that a simple list may not.
Especially do not leave blank lines between items in a list, since this causes the MediaWiki software to misinterpret each item as beginning a new list.
(There are HTML techniques to insert linebreaks or additional paragraphs into a list item.)  Avoid misuse of list markup in articles for visual styling of non-list material.

Should the length of the list items or the topical relevance of said image discourage display at the top corner, consider placing it after the asterisk of the first list-item it illustrates (as in example "C") to avoid breaking continuity of the unordered list () element.
dos: The items on these lists might include links to specific articles or other information, and must be supported with references like any article.
Embedded lists should be used only when appropriate; sometimes the information in a list is better presented as prose.

Ensure that list items have the same importance to the subject as would be required for the item to be included in the text of the article, according to Wikipedia policies and guidelines (including WP:Trivia sections).
For example, list of Indian language poets should link to both list of Indians and list of poets.
There is some controversy over how many links to articles and links to lists that should be put in any article.
Some feel the optimum number of links to lists that should be included at the end of any given article is zero, one, or two.
In general, when deciding what list to include, the same criteria used to decide what articles to include in the "See also" section should be used.
Editors should try to put themselves in the readers' frame of mind and ask "Where will I likely want to go after reading this article?".

However, as Wikipedia is optimized for readers over editors, any lists which exist primarily for development or maintenance purposes (such as a list that consists entirely of red links and does not serve an informational purpose; especially a list of missing topics) should be in either the project or user space, not the main space.

Additionally, an overly precise list title can be less useful and can make the list difficult to find; the precise inclusion criteria for the list should be spelled out in the lead section (see below), not the title.
To suggest that a list in an article or section should be sorted, use .
Prose is preferred in articles because it allows the presentation of detail and clarification of context in a way that a simple list may not.

style="background:#bfffbf;" | C (good)
| <syntaxhighlight lang="wikitext" style="margin:0px; background:#cfffcf;">
 * Example 1
 * Example 2
 * thumb|Caption text Example 3
 * Example 4
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

To float pictures to the right of the list, one should put the image markup before the first item in most cases, see the example "A".

=== Format list items consistently === List items should be formatted consistently in a list. Unless there is a good reason to use different list types in the same page, consistency throughout an article is also desirable.

=== Introductory material ===


Lists should have introductory material; for stand-alone lists, this should be the lead section.
donts: The content of a list is governed by the same content policies as prose, including principles of due weight and avoiding original research.
As a general rule, the "See also" section should not repeat links that appear in the article's body.
*Dynamic list – a dynamic list is any list that may never be fully complete (since it will only include notable examples from a given category), or may require constant updates to remain current.

If users have some general idea of what they are looking for but do not know the specific terminology, they could browse the lists of basic topics and more comprehensive lists of topics, which in turn lead to most if not all of Wikipedia's lists, which in turn lead to related articles.
The list title should not be misleading and should normally not include abbreviations.
Note that the word "famous" is considered an unnecessary  "peacock" embellishment and should not be used.
Especially do not leave blank lines between items in a list, since this causes the MediaWiki software to misinterpret each item as beginning a new list.
(There are HTML techniques to insert linebreaks or additional paragraphs into a list item.)  Avoid misuse of list markup in articles for visual styling of non-list material.

Should the length of the list items or the topical relevance of said image discourage display at the top corner, consider placing it after the asterisk of the first list-item it illustrates (as in example "C") to avoid breaking continuity of the unordered list () element.
Use the same grammatical form for all items in a list – avoid mixing sentences and sentence fragments as items.
A list item should not end with a full stop unless it consists of a complete sentence or is the end of a list that forms one.

It is not required, however, and should not be used for a list that is not the focus of a section, or for lists in an article that uses a lot of short lists and which is better arranged by more topical headings that group related lists.

Lists should never contain "Unsorted" or "Miscellaneous" headings, as all items worthy of inclusion in the list can be sorted by some criteria, although it is entirely possible that the formatting of the list would need to be revamped to include all appropriate items.
* Prefer using full sentences, and avoid mixing sentences and fragments as items in the same list.

Do not insert blank lines between vertical list items.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Portals
rules.label: Manual of Style/Portals
key_statements: While portals are not in the main (article) namespace, they also contain reader-facing content; therefore, they should generally follow the MoS guidelines, with some alterations as explained on this page.
However, some of the provisions of the Wikipedia Manual of Style do not make sense for portals, because they organize other content rather than communicating information themselves.

However, use common sense: introductory material should be at or near the top, and external and sister project links should be at or near the bottom (or in a utility sidebar, depending on page layout).
Layout should be usable on mobile view, as this has become a very popular access method.
Banner images should either fit the page width or box width, or remain within the bounds of the visible page.

They should be compatible with mobile view.{{efn|1=Mobile view can be tested on a desktop system, with the "Mobile sidebar preview" option under "Testing and development" at Special:Preferences#mw-prefsection-gadgets.

This requires the Vector (default) skin.}}

Other exceptions may be needed from time to time as the MoS evolves, so that portals can best fulfill their purpose; these should be discussed and added to this page if there is consensus to do so.
dos: While portals are not in the main (article) namespace, they also contain reader-facing content; therefore, they should generally follow the MoS guidelines, with some alterations as explained on this page.

However, use common sense: introductory material should be at or near the top, and external and sister project links should be at or near the bottom (or in a utility sidebar, depending on page layout).
Layout should be usable on mobile view, as this has become a very popular access method.

These should follow the MoS guidelines, including those on capital letters and dashes, as well as the applicable criteria in the article titles policy, and any relevant topical naming-convention guidelines.
Banner images should either fit the page width or box width, or remain within the bounds of the visible page.

They should be compatible with mobile view.{{efn|1=Mobile view can be tested on a desktop system, with the "Mobile sidebar preview" option under "Testing and development" at Special:Preferences#mw-prefsection-gadgets.

This requires the Vector (default) skin.}}

Other exceptions may be needed from time to time as the MoS evolves, so that portals can best fulfill their purpose; these should be discussed and added to this page if there is consensus to do so.
donts: However, some of the provisions of the Wikipedia Manual of Style do not make sense for portals, because they organize other content rather than communicating information themselves.

==Alterations== ===Layout=== and Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Layout}} Portals are not articles, and do not require sections to be separated by heading markup (==Heading==); the most common approach and similar templates}} uses headings within a box layout.

Section headings in portals should be treated as heading-like material, and as such should follow the section heading advice regarding sentence case, redundancy, images, and questions, where this does not adversely affect their utility to the reader.<!--mainly regarding redundancy-->

Portals need to follow Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility, including in choice of colours with sufficient contrast, readable font sizing, and avoiding misuse of tables for layout.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Record charts
rules.label: Manual of Style/Record charts
key_statements: Specifics about which charts should be included are common to the tables, article prose, and discography tables, and guidance about what charts to include may be found in WP:Record charts.
The chart positions should be organized into one table, and the table should be formatted using class="wikitable sortable".
Charts should be arranged by country in alphabetical order.

Within the table, charts should be properly named as listed by the source and peaks should not appear boldfaced, as this violates Wikipedia's policy regarding neutral point of view and breaches WP:MOSBOLD.
Flag icons should not be displayed on chart tables, as per MOS:FLAG, nationalities should not be emphasized.

(These are not legitimate references for information contained in this article.) References should be as specific as possible, and generally should be to a URL that will display the exact charting information being included, not simply to the website where the chart may be found.
Thus sources should be archived meaning that they can be accessed at later dates.
Where possible 'outside' archiving is also preferred.
Articles about songs and albums should not include succession boxes that indicate which songs or albums preceded and succeeded it on the top spot of a chart.

== Original (manual) chart format ==
Note that references should be individual and specific to each chart that is being used.
dos: Specifics about which charts should be included are common to the tables, article prose, and discography tables, and guidance about what charts to include may be found in WP:Record charts.
The chart positions should be organized into one table, and the table should be formatted using class="wikitable sortable".
Charts should be arranged by country in alphabetical order.

(These are not legitimate references for information contained in this article.) References should be as specific as possible, and generally should be to a URL that will display the exact charting information being included, not simply to the website where the chart may be found.
Thus sources should be archived meaning that they can be accessed at later dates.
Where possible 'outside' archiving is also preferred.

== Original (manual) chart format ==
Note that references should be individual and specific to each chart that is being used.

Alternative versions of the same song, such as remixes or radio edits, should be indicated in the table as an additional line within the Chart name cell: {|class="wikitable plainrowheaders sortable" style="text-align:center;" border="1" !scope="col"|Chart (2007) !scope="col"|Peak
position |- !scope="row"|Australian Singles Chart |1 |- !scope="row"|German Singles Chart *German radio version |2 |- !scope="row"|UK Singles Chart *Extended dance remix |3 |- !scope="row"|US Billboard Hot 100 |4 |- !scope="row"|US Mainstream Rock Tracks |5 |} == Chart templates == The template is available for formatting chart tables for single articles and the template is for album articles.

The templates will look something like this on pages: {|class="wikitable plainrowheaders sortable" border="1" !scope="col"|Chart (2008) !scope="col"|Peak
position |- |- |- |- |- |- |- |- |- |} The references will look like this: Notes *For the German template you must format the artist name "last,first" and the song must be written like it is on their website for example "I Want to Know What Love Is (2-track)".
donts: Within the table, charts should be properly named as listed by the source and peaks should not appear boldfaced, as this violates Wikipedia's policy regarding neutral point of view and breaches WP:MOSBOLD.
Flag icons should not be displayed on chart tables, as per MOS:FLAG, nationalities should not be emphasized.
Articles about songs and albums should not include succession boxes that indicate which songs or albums preceded and succeeded it on the top spot of a chart.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Glossaries
rules.label: Manual of Style/Glossaries
sections: How to handle multiple variants of a term
key_statements:
Like everything else on Wikipedia, the existence of the term must be verifiable.}}

{{defn|1=Prose that addresses the term of the entry in an encyclopedically descriptive manner.
Do not make individual terms in a template-structured glossary into headings.
Use the templates as a set, and do not mix-and-match glossary templates with wikimarkup description list code (; and : style) or other markup.

The flexibility and power of HTML tags are much richer than those provided by wikimarkup's ; and : description list or * unordered list features, which do not work properly due to MediaWiki bugs and misfeatures.
Within a , all text and other content must be  a .
Numbered lists (beginning with # instead of *) should not be used, as they imply a specific (e.g.
The  markup is required both before and after the break or the line spacing will be noticeably inconsistent.
Multiple definitions are numbered manually, as shown; do not use ordered lists.
Entries must not be added randomly or in an arbitrary order.
Numeric entries that sometimes appear in the article subject's context as either numerals or spelled out verbally should be given in the form "three (3)" in short glossaries.

If there are no symbolic and only one or a few numeral entries, it is permissible to alphabetize the numeral entries as if they were spelled out verbally, in the form "3 (three)", to avoid creation of a very short numeral entries section.

Do not section an embedded list, as this impedes editing and may greatly lengthen the overall article's table of contents; if an embedded list is long enough that it would benefit from sectioning, it is a good candidate splitting into a separate stand-alone glossary article.
All entries must be verifiable with reliable sources, just like regular article content.
If both versions do exist they should link to each other in their respective "See also" sections with a sister-project template, e.g.
}} (see Wikipedia:Wikimedia sister projects for recommendations about the best choice of template for various situations).
In template-structured glossaries, the bare term, without markup, must be the first parameter of , and the language-markup version is parameter 2.
In most cases, multiple definitions should be numbered.

Because articles may use numbers to distinguish among multiple definitions, avoid changing the order of definitions without good reason, and correct numerical references to definitions when you do change their order.

===Linking===
====Outgoing links====
Wikilinks to other pages or sections should generally not be used within the term in a description list, whether formatted with glossary templates or not.
Linking from inside a term should especially be avoided in actual glossaries, where the term's entry should stand on its own within that context.
dos: {{Nutshell|1=Stand-alone glossary list articles, as well as in-article embedded glossaries, should be formatted in a consistent manner.

Like everything else on Wikipedia, the existence of the term must be verifiable.}}

{{defn|1=Prose that addresses the term of the entry in an encyclopedically descriptive manner.
Within a , all text and other content must be  a .
The  markup is required both before and after the break or the line spacing will be noticeably inconsistent.
|} ==General guidelines for writing glossaries == The following guidelines apply to all glossaries and should be followed regardless of which format style presented above is used.
Numeric entries that sometimes appear in the article subject's context as either numerals or spelled out verbally should be given in the form "three (3)" in short glossaries.
All entries must be verifiable with reliable sources, just like regular article content.
If both versions do exist they should link to each other in their respective "See also" sections with a sister-project template, e.g.
}} (see Wikipedia:Wikimedia sister projects for recommendations about the best choice of template for various situations).
In template-structured glossaries, the bare term, without markup, must be the first parameter of , and the language-markup version is parameter 2.
In most cases, multiple definitions should be numbered.

===Linking===
====Outgoing links====
Wikilinks to other pages or sections should generally not be used within the term in a description list, whether formatted with glossary templates or not.
Images can be added to the definition, but must be inline in it, without a link break.
In template-structured glossaries, hatnotes, images, and other such content must be placed within the definition – not between term and definition, nor between the last definition and the next term.
Existing examples should be converted to template-structured glossaries, since most of the work is already done.
donts: Do not make individual terms in a template-structured glossary into headings.
Use the templates as a set, and do not mix-and-match glossary templates with wikimarkup description list code (; and : style) or other markup.

The flexibility and power of HTML tags are much richer than those provided by wikimarkup's ; and : description list or * unordered list features, which do not work properly due to MediaWiki bugs and misfeatures.
Numbered lists (beginning with # instead of *) should not be used, as they imply a specific (e.g.
Multiple definitions are numbered manually, as shown; do not use ordered lists.
Entries must not be added randomly or in an arbitrary order.

If there are no symbolic and only one or a few numeral entries, it is permissible to alphabetize the numeral entries as if they were spelled out verbally, in the form "3 (three)", to avoid creation of a very short numeral entries section.

Do not section an embedded list, as this impedes editing and may greatly lengthen the overall article's table of contents; if an embedded list is long enough that it would benefit from sectioning, it is a good candidate splitting into a separate stand-alone glossary article.
Do not create empty sections.

The use of leading capitals on all entries is recommended  if all of the following apply simultaneously:
*No term in the glossary is a lower-case form of something that in other contexts is usually capitalized
*There is no difference between the capitalized and uncapitalized form of any term in the glossary
*The terms do not contain a mixture of proper names and common nouns
*Capitalizing all entries is otherwise unlikely to produce any form of ambiguity or confusion.
Do not add everyday words.

Because articles may use numbers to distinguish among multiple definitions, avoid changing the order of definitions without good reason, and correct numerical references to definitions when you do change their order.
Linking from inside a term should especially be avoided in actual glossaries, where the term's entry should stand on its own within that context.
===Avoid semicolon-and-colon wikimarkup for glossaries=== (with ; and :) for glossaries, as it is severely limited and buggy.

Consensus discussions on this at Wikipedia talk:Article size have repeatedly come to the conclusion that splitting of lists like glossaries should be avoided if possible because it vastly reduces their utility, searchability, and editability.

key_statements: ==General guidelines== Subscripts and superscripts should be wrapped in and below).
Do not use the ²}} and , or [[List of XML and HTML character entity references|XML/HTML character entity references ( etc.).
Tone should usually be marked with diacritics or IPA tone symbols, according to Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Pronunciation#Tone.

===Orthographies===
Superscript and subscript symbols in orthographies of languages, such as in the Proto-Indo-European language, some Salishan languages, and the Wade–Giles romanization of Mandarin, should use Unicode subscripts and superscripts.
Formulae formatted without using TeX should use the same syntax to maintain the same appearance.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Contents
rules.label: Manual of Style/Contents
key_statements:
* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility 
** Details recommended editing practices for best web accessibility of Wikipedia.

* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Self-references to avoid 
** How to avoid mentioning Wikipedia itself, or the fact the article is a webpage.

* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Trivia sections 
** How to avoid accumulation of poorly formatted miscellaneous information.
* Avoiding common mistakes – gives a list of common mistakes and how to avoid them.
dos:
It contains general remarks, summaries of the guidance to be found on certain other MoS pages, and fully detailed guidance on certain topics:
** How to deal with national varieties of English 
** Language for section headings 
** How to format quotations 
** The preferred usage of punctuation , the largest section specific to the main Manual of Style page
** Miscellaneous grammatical issues including the writing of possessives, use of pronouns, the ampersand , collective plurals , and general guidelines for dealing with non-English words and languages.

* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility 
** Details recommended editing practices for best web accessibility of Wikipedia.

* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers 
** Covers time and date formatting, including seasons , recommendations for numbers and their notation, which units to use and how to format and abbreviate them , dealing with currencies  and geographical coordinates .
donts:
* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Self-references to avoid 
** How to avoid mentioning Wikipedia itself, or the fact the article is a webpage.

* Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Trivia sections 
** How to avoid accumulation of poorly formatted miscellaneous information.
* Avoiding common mistakes – gives a list of common mistakes and how to avoid them.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Chemistry/Safety
rules.label: Manual of Style/Chemistry/Safety
key_statements:
{{nutshell|
*Safety sections should only be included when the information adds something to the article.
*It is almost never appropriate to use primary sources in writing a safety section.
Reliable secondary sources should be used instead.
*Regulations, when noteworthy, should not be enumerated.
When the safety section is warranted beyond the information in the Chembox, information should be succinctly presented.
Obvious hazards that depend on knowledge of basic chemistry do not warrant inclusion.
For example, since hexafluorophosphoric acid is a strong acid, it is not necessary to state that it should be stored away from bases and reactive metals.
The description of hazards should avoid anecdotes.
Hazards should be peer reviewed, and not taken from newspaper accounts.
Descriptions of hazards should, as far as possible, be based on published, peer-reviewed sources, which should be cited at the appropriate point in the article.
Prudence must be exercised in covering such regulatory topics.
If the compound is a drug, follow WikiProject Pharmacology's recommendations.
These are a preferred source when discussing human carcinogenicity.
They do not include citations to the original literature, but are peer-reviewed.
Although they are usually competently complied, peer-reviewed sources are always better.
Which version should we choose as our source?
*They are commercial documents, usually held on the supplier's website: why should we favour one supplier over another in linking to their sites?
dos:
{{nutshell|
*Safety sections should only be included when the information adds something to the article.
Reliable secondary sources should be used instead.
When the safety section is warranted beyond the information in the Chembox, information should be succinctly presented.
For example, since hexafluorophosphoric acid is a strong acid, it is not necessary to state that it should be stored away from bases and reactive metals.
Hazards should be peer reviewed, and not taken from newspaper accounts.
Descriptions of hazards should, as far as possible, be based on published, peer-reviewed sources, which should be cited at the appropriate point in the article.
Prudence must be exercised in covering such regulatory topics.
If the compound is a drug, follow WikiProject Pharmacology's recommendations.
These are a preferred source when discussing human carcinogenicity.

;National Toxicology Program
The National Toxicology Program of the Department of Health and Human Services publishes a regular Report on Carcinogens: the most recent available version is the [http://ntp.niehs.nih.gov/index.cfm?objectid=32BA9724-F1F6-975E-7FCE50709CB4C932 11th ed.] The IARC Monographs (see above) are preferred as a source for carcinogenicity information, as they cover more substances in greater depth, but the NTP report is also reliable.
Although they are usually competently complied, peer-reviewed sources are always better.
Which version should we choose as our source?
*They are commercial documents, usually held on the supplier's website: why should we favour one supplier over another in linking to their sites?
donts: *It is almost never appropriate to use primary sources in writing a safety section.
*Regulations, when noteworthy, should not be enumerated.
Obvious hazards that depend on knowledge of basic chemistry do not warrant inclusion.
The description of hazards should avoid anecdotes.

==Coverage of regulations==
In the usual encyclopedic way, the Project avoids serving either as a safety manual or a guide to regulation.<ref>An illustrative discussion: Wikipedia talk:WikiProject Chemicals/Archive 2011#Recommend New Template or Project</ref>  We follow the advice in WP:NOTGUIDE and WP:NOTMANUAL.

As indicated by What Wikipedia is not, editors are discouraged from providing advice about poisons or emergencies associated with chemical compounds: "a Wikipedia article should not read like a how-to style manual of instructions, advice (legal, medical, or otherwise) or suggestions."

== Sources of safety information ==
===Secondary sources (UN agencies)===
;United Nations specialized agencies (WHO, ILO, UNEP) with the collaboration of many other national and international bodies.
They do not include citations to the original literature, but are peer-reviewed.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Indonesia-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Indonesia-related articles
key_statements: The purpose is to avoid inappropriate disambiguations and moving/redirecting articles.
* All regencies should have "Regency" term at the end to avoid disambiguation with city or other names.
There is no exception for this because this is always the name of regency addressed officially in Indonesia.
* All geographical directions should be translated into English.

== Ethnic groups and languages ==
Names of languages should follow Wikipedia:Naming conventions (languages).
Per Wikipedia:Naming conventions (languages), the Indonesian language should be referred to as "Indonesian" or "the Indonesian language" if necessary.

== Spelling ==
In general, Indonesian words should be spelled according to the latest spelling system (e.g.
Variant spellings should be noted in the article, especially if they remain in common use, and there should be redirects from them.
In general, the Indonesian form of words is preferred for concepts that are used in more than one ethnic region (e.g.
If a concept is unique to a particular cultural area, the spelling of the word should follow the local usage only if it is more common in English in that spelling.
If the local spelling is to be used, the current spelling conventions should be followed.

For example, for Javanese retroflex consonants, "dh" and "th" should be used instead of "ḍ" and "ṭ."

In Javanese, the letter "a" is sometimes pronounced like "o" in the final syllables of words, and this is often reflected in the spelling.
If the spelling varies depending on source, this variation should be noted in the article.
In these cases, for living people their most recent name should be used in article titles, unless a former name remains considerably more common in the English-speaking world.
For dead people, whichever name is the most common in English-language discussions should be used, even if it was not the person's final name.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Portuguese-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Portuguese-related articles
key_statements:
When they first occur in an article, names should be included like this:

"Assembleia da República" (Assembly of the Republic - the Portuguese parliament).
In the same fashion, titles of works of art should not be translated; their titles may be explained, just as is done with other names.
In such a case, the name it was published under is the one that should be used to name the article about it.
The Portuguese title should be included in the article's text along with an explanation of its meaning, if it is significantly different from the one used on the English edition.
dos:
Normally the names will be arranged in this fashion:

Given1 Given2 Mother'sMotherLast Mother'sFatherLast Father'sMotherLast Father'sFatherLast Husband'sLast

Any combination of this can be used, the most common nowadays being:

Given1 Given2 Mother'sLast Father'sLast

The appendages "de" "da" "do" and "dos" all mean "of" or "from" and should be included, except when naming someone only by last name:

: José de Almeida is correct

:Mr.

When they first occur in an article, names should be included like this:

"Assembleia da República" (Assembly of the Republic - the Portuguese parliament).
In such a case, the name it was published under is the one that should be used to name the article about it.
The Portuguese title should be included in the article's text along with an explanation of its meaning, if it is significantly different from the one used on the English edition.
donts:
==Translation of names==
Whenever a commonly used English equivalent for a Portuguese language name does not exist, one should not be created:

"Assembleia da República" should not be translated as "Assembly of the Republic", not only because it's not its name, but also because it makes it difficult for someone doing further research, as the proper name is missing.
In the same fashion, titles of works of art should not be translated; their titles may be explained, just as is done with other names.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Mathematics
rules.label: Manual of Style/Mathematics
sections: Writing style in mathematics
key_statements:
===Article introduction===


Articles should start with a short introductory section, called the "lead".
The lead should, as much as possible, be accessible to a general reader, so specialized terminology and symbols should be avoided.
Formulas should appear in the first paragraph only if necessary, since  they will not be displayed in the preview that pops up when hovering over a link.
For having formulae displayed when hovering, they must be written in raw html (without templates  or ), or in  (inside ).
In general, the lead sentence should include the article title, or some variation thereof, in bold along with any alternate names, also in bold.
The lead sentence should state that the article is about a topic in mathematics, unless the title already does so.
The lead sentence should informally define or describe the subject.
The origin of the subject's name should be explained if it is not self-evident.
The appropriate audience for the overview will vary by article, but it should be as basic as reasonable.
The informal introduction should clearly state that it is informal, and that it is only stated to introduce the formal approach.
When in doubt, articles should define the notation they use.
For example, some readers will immediately recognize that Δ(K) means the discriminant of a number field, but others will never have encountered the notation.
If an article requires non-standard or uncommon notation, they should be defined.
For example, an article that uses x^n or x**n to denote exponentiation (instead of x<sup>n</sup>) should define the notations.
An article about a mathematical object should provide an exact definition of the object, perhaps in a "Definition" section after section(s) of motivation.

The phrase "formal definition" may help to flag the actual definition of a concept for readers unfamiliar with academic terminology, in which "definition" means formal definition, and a "proof" is always a formal proof.
When the topic is a theorem, the article should provide a precise statement of the theorem.
In keeping with the purpose and tone of an encyclopedia, examples should be informative rather than instructional (see WP:NOTTEXTBOOK for details).
Formulas tend to repel less mathematical readers, and mathematics articles should take pains to explain (or even replace) them by words if possible.
In particular, the English words "for all", "exists", and "in" should be preferred to the corresponding symbols ∀, ∃, and ∈.
dos:
===Article introduction===


Articles should start with a short introductory section, called the "lead".
Formulas should appear in the first paragraph only if necessary, since  they will not be displayed in the preview that pops up when hovering over a link.
For having formulae displayed when hovering, they must be written in raw html (without templates  or ), or in  (inside ).
In general, the lead sentence should include the article title, or some variation thereof, in bold along with any alternate names, also in bold.
The lead sentence should state that the article is about a topic in mathematics, unless the title already does so.
The lead sentence should informally define or describe the subject.

For example:
In Greek , and ) is concerned with the properties of a geometric object that are preserved under continuous deformations, such as stretching, twisting, crumpling and bending, but not tearing or gluing.
In Euclidean plane geometry, Apollonius's problem is to construct circles that are tangent to three given circles in a plane.
The lead section should include, when appropriate: *Historical motivation, including names and dates, especially if the article does not have a "History" section.
The origin of the subject's name should be explained if it is not self-evident.
The appropriate audience for the overview will vary by article, but it should be as basic as reasonable.
The informal introduction should clearly state that it is informal, and that it is only stated to introduce the formal approach.
When in doubt, articles should define the notation they use.
If an article requires non-standard or uncommon notation, they should be defined.
For example, an article that uses x^n or x**n to denote exponentiation (instead of x<sup>n</sup>) should define the notations.
An article about a mathematical object should provide an exact definition of the object, perhaps in a "Definition" section after section(s) of motivation.

The phrase "formal definition" may help to flag the actual definition of a concept for readers unfamiliar with academic terminology, in which "definition" means formal definition, and a "proof" is always a formal proof.
donts: The lead should, as much as possible, be accessible to a general reader, so specialized terminology and symbols should be avoided.
For example, some readers will immediately recognize that Δ(K) means the discriminant of a number field, but others will never have encountered the notation.
In the interest of clarity, sentences should not begin with a symbol.

Do not write:
* {{!xt|Suppose that G is a group.

While opinions vary on the most edifying style, authors should generally provide more than bare lists of facts and formulae, while avoiding textbook-style rhetorical devices like leading questions and opinionated statements like .
Also avoid contentless clichés as , , , , , and .
Whenever a variable or other symbol is defined by a formula, make sure to say this is a definition introducing a notation, not an equation involving a previously known object.
When defining a term, do not use the phrase "if and only if".

For example, instead of
* 
write
* 

If it is reasonable to do so, rephrase the sentence to avoid the use of the word "if" entirely.

For example,
* 



Avoid, as far as possible, useless phrases such as:
* 
* 
* 

The reader might not find what you write obvious.

Instead, try to hint why something must hold, such as:
* 
* 

Articles should avoid common blackboard abbreviations such as wrt (with respect to), wlog (without loss of generality), and iff (if and only if), as well as quantifier symbols ∀ and ∃ instead of for all and there exists.
Avoid any when verbalizing quantifiers since it is ambiguous.
Thus the act of changing an article from one set of conventions to another should not be undertaken lightly.

A structure satisfying all the ring axioms except the existence of a multiplicative identity is called a rng.<ref>Currently, ring (mathematics) and related articles attempt to cover both unital rings and non-unital rings: they do not consistently implement this interpretation.

This attempt to cover multiple meanings violates WP:DICT#Major differences (homographs).</ref> There is an exception for rings of operators, such as * algebras, B* algebras, C* algebras, which we do not assume to be unital.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Ethiopia-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Ethiopia-related articles
key_statements: The following rules do not claim to be the last word.
He should not be referred to as "Zara" or "Mr Yaqob" -- unless this unusual usage can be verified in a reliable source, which then needs to be added to the relevant article.
Ethiopians, especially in the West, will frequently adopt their own transliteration scheme for styling themselves, that does not always correspond to any of the various schemes employed by linguists.
Whenever proper names become notable under such ad hoc spellings, they should be preferred as the most common searchable term, e.g.
When transliterating a name, the article should first be at the name most commonly used in English.
Abba Bahrey at Bahrey, but Bahr negus Yeshaq at Bahr negus Yeshaq, as he is known commonly with the title.<!-- note - that should be Bahr NEGASH Yeshaq, not Negus.

-->

Articles should begin with this common name (in bold), followed with a parenthetical notation containing the following:

*First in the parentheses, following the primary spelling, should be any other significant English spelling variants.
Immediately after the complex transliteration should come any English transliterations when available and applicable.
If the word's primary meaning is in Ge'ez, then a comma should follow the complex transliteration followed by Ge'ez</nowiki>).

For words important out of a Ge'ez context (and pronounced differently or with a different meaning), the applicable languages' pronunciation using the complex transliteration (and meaning, if different), should follow.
Using the full language name before the different transliteration is not prohibited, but it is recommended that the abbreviation of the language be used for non-Ge'ez languages.
*After the primary word and its transliteration and translation should come any other very common native language variants in bold.
"Gudit," "Yodit," and "Isato" [or Esato] are all variants on a single Jewish/pagan queen in the 9th/10th century and should be bolded.
The variants are within Amharic itself rather than due to differences in transliteration system, but should still be included in bold as the first notation in parentheses.
Following these alternatives, transliteration should be provided as above.

===In text===

This edition of Wikipedia is in English, so do not use the Ge'ez alphabet or its transliteration excessively, such as for common nouns.
Where it is used, the name in Ge'ez letters, followed by its complex transliteration, both in parentheses, should directly follow the first (and only the first) mention of the name.
Again, to help establish a simple and clean appearance, if a term is already hyperlinked to an article with Ge'ez text and romanization, please do not provide the characters or romanization again.

It should instead be rendered as:

: ...his older brother Tewodros ordered Zara Yaqob confined...
In general, non-English insertions should be in parentheses to minimize interruption to the flow of reading.

When transliterating a name, the article should first be at the name most commonly used in English.
Abba Bahrey at Bahrey, but Bahr negus Yeshaq at Bahr negus Yeshaq, as he is known commonly with the title.<!-- note - that should be Bahr NEGASH Yeshaq, not Negus.

-->

Articles should begin with this common name (in bold), followed with a parenthetical notation containing the following:

*First in the parentheses, following the primary spelling, should be any other significant English spelling variants.

*For articles where a Ge'ez transliteration of the title is provided: Second in the parentheses, following a semicolon ";", should be the word Ge'ez</nowiki>), followed by the primary name in Ge'ez script and a complex transliteration as per Wikipedia:WikiProject Ethiopia/Transliteration.
Immediately after the complex transliteration should come any English transliterations when available and applicable.
If the word's primary meaning is in Ge'ez, then a comma should follow the complex transliteration followed by Ge'ez</nowiki>).

For words important out of a Ge'ez context (and pronounced differently or with a different meaning), the applicable languages' pronunciation using the complex transliteration (and meaning, if different), should follow.

(Some of those abbreviations are Amh., Gur., Gz., Orm., Som., Tgn., and Tgr., for Amharic, Oromo, Somali, Tigrinya, and Tigre.) After the transliteration of the other word terms (with Ge'ez alphabet spelling first if different from Ge'ez spelling) should come the meaning if different from the Ge'ez one, available, and applicable.
*After the primary word and its transliteration and translation should come any other very common native language variants in bold.
"Gudit," "Yodit," and "Isato" [or Esato] are all variants on a single Jewish/pagan queen in the 9th/10th century and should be bolded.
The variants are within Amharic itself rather than due to differences in transliteration system, but should still be included in bold as the first notation in parentheses.
Following these alternatives, transliteration should be provided as above.
Where it is used, the name in Ge'ez letters, followed by its complex transliteration, both in parentheses, should directly follow the first (and only the first) mention of the name.

It should instead be rendered as:

: ...his older brother Tewodros ordered Zara Yaqob confined...

Whenever proper names become notable under such ad hoc spellings, they should be preferred as the most common searchable term, e.g.
Using the full language name before the different transliteration is not prohibited, but it is recommended that the abbreviation of the language be used for non-Ge'ez languages.

===In text===

This edition of Wikipedia is in English, so do not use the Ge'ez alphabet or its transliteration excessively, such as for common nouns.
Again, to help establish a simple and clean appearance, if a term is already hyperlinked to an article with Ge'ez text and romanization, please do not provide the characters or romanization again.

dos:
If you disagree with any of the conventions, please discuss in the talk page.

For more specific guidelines relating to Northern Ireland and historical Ireland, see Wikipedia:Manual of Style (Ireland-related articles).

==Settlements==

* When detailing a settlement, such as the birthplace of a notable person or the headquarters of an organisation, the county (if applicable) and constituent country of the UK should be stated.
* In cases where there is no applicable county, such as some unitary authorities, then the county should be omitted.
* Where it is required to display a flag representing the settlement, the flag of the constituent country should  follow the Union Flag in accordance with standard flag-display rules.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Medicine-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Medicine-related articles
sections: Wikipedia is not a medical primary resource
key_statements:
* Avoid trivia sections and external link farms.}}



This is the style guide for editing medical articles.
Most biologics, including vaccines, do not have INN or other generic names, so the brand name is used instead.

===Titles requiring disambiguation===


When one single term is used to describe two different topics, article titles must be disambiguated to resolve naming conflicts.
When the medical or anatomical context is the primary use of the term, the article should not use a disambiguation word.
In this situation, the general medical specialty (for medicine) or specific body part (for anatomy) should be used.
Although healthcare professionals and patients may find much of interest, these two groups do not by themselves represent the target audience.
* You give advice, particularly when medical help should be sought or is required.
* You use a writing style appropriate for 12-year-olds, because the sixth grade reading level is recommended for patient information leaflets.
Instead, you should maintain an international perspective, e.g., by seeking out sources about non-English-speaking countries, or using examples from a variety of countries.

===Trivia===


Avoid lists of trivia; anything worth including should be integrated into the main body text.
Articles with sections on Research directions should be sourced to reviews and should describe research directions, rather than discussing specific studies and their results.
Articles about health and medicine should generally not cite primary sources, more details about which can be found at WP:MEDRS.

(See the Manual of Style.) In addition, note that:
* Technical terms
** Approved and indicated mean different things and should not be used interchangeably.
** Do not confuse patient-group prevalence figures with those for the whole population that have a certain condition.
For example: "One third of XYZ patients" is not always the same as "One third of people with XYZ", since many people with XYZ may not be seeking medical care.

If the source talks about the effect of a single chemical purified from a plant (e.g., aspirin from white willow bark), do not misrepresent the work as referring to the whole plant from which it was derived.
Similarly, if the source talks about effects in cultured cells or non-human animals, do not misrepresent the work as demonstrating anything about humans.
** Avoid using the potentially ambiguous term doctor to refer specifically to physicians or surgeons.
Avoid using doctor or physician in ways that incorrectly exclude other licensed healthcare professionals, such as nurses, physician assistants, and midwives.
Avoid using allopathic to describe modern Western medicine; instead use conventional medicine or mainstream medicine.
dos:
==Article titles==



The article title should be the scientific or recognised medical name that is most commonly used in recent, high-quality, English-language medical sources, rather than a lay term (unscientific or slang name)<ref name="namingissue">This convention was documented at the now-defunct WikiProject Clinical medicine,[https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikipedia:WikiProject_Clinical_medicine&oldid=172855825#The_naming_issue] and was the result of several discussions in 2004.[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia_talk:Naming_conventions_(common_names)/Archive_01#Naming_conventions_for_medicine] [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia_talk:Naming_conventions/Archive_4#Medicine] [https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikipedia:Village_pump_archive_2004-09-26&oldid=3620313#The_names_of_diseases:_policy]</ref> or an historical laundry lists by spammers; encyclopaedic, sourced content such as "many generic brand names exist" is preferred.</ref> Create redirects to the article to help those searching with alternative names.

Where there is a dispute over a name, editors should cite recognised authorities and organisations rather than conduct original research.<ref name=NameOR>Examples of original research include counting Google or PubMed results, comparing the size or relevance of the varieties of English, and quoting from personal or professional experience.</ref>

Where there are lexical differences between the varieties of English, an international standard may be helpful, though Wikipedia generally discourages changing existing article usage.

Nonproprietary names are common nouns and hence should be lower case except in titles, the beginning of sentences, and in other situations that require capitalization.<ref>{{cite book | author = CBE Style Manual Committee | title = Scientific Style and Format: the CBE Manual for Authors, Editors and Publishers | date = 2002 | publisher=Cambridge Univ.

Press | location = Cambridge | isbn = 978-0-521-47154-1 | pages = 324 | edition = 6th | chapter = Chaper 19: Drugs and Pharmacokinetics | chapter-url =https://books.google.com/books?id=PoFJ-OhE63UC&q=nonproprietary+name+capitalization&pg=PA324}}</ref> In contrast, trade names are proper nouns and should always be capitalised.

===Titles requiring disambiguation===


When one single term is used to describe two different topics, article titles must be disambiguated to resolve naming conflicts.
In this situation, the general medical specialty (for medicine) or specific body part (for anatomy) should be used.
* You give advice, particularly when medical help should be sought or is required.
* You use a writing style appropriate for 12-year-olds, because the sixth grade reading level is recommended for patient information leaflets.
Instead, you should maintain an international perspective, e.g., by seeking out sources about non-English-speaking countries, or using examples from a variety of countries.
Articles with sections on Research directions should be sourced to reviews and should describe research directions, rather than discussing specific studies and their results.
Articles about health and medicine should generally not cite primary sources, more details about which can be found at WP:MEDRS.
For example: "One third of XYZ patients" is not always the same as "One third of people with XYZ", since many people with XYZ may not be seeking medical care.
Words like disease, disorder, or affliction are not always appropriate.

==== Suicide and self-harm ====


Suicide and self-harm should be written about in an encyclopaedic tone, without undue emphasis.
** All deaths should be reported with equal brevity and clarity.
donts:
* Avoid trivia sections and external link farms.}}



This is the style guide for editing medical articles.
Most biologics, including vaccines, do not have INN or other generic names, so the brand name is used instead.
When the medical or anatomical context is the primary use of the term, the article should not use a disambiguation word.
Although healthcare professionals and patients may find much of interest, these two groups do not by themselves represent the target audience.

===Trivia===


Avoid lists of trivia; anything worth including should be integrated into the main body text.

(See the Manual of Style.) In addition, note that:
* Technical terms
** Approved and indicated mean different things and should not be used interchangeably.
** Do not confuse patient-group prevalence figures with those for the whole population that have a certain condition.

If the source talks about the effect of a single chemical purified from a plant (e.g., aspirin from white willow bark), do not misrepresent the work as referring to the whole plant from which it was derived.
Similarly, if the source talks about effects in cultured cells or non-human animals, do not misrepresent the work as demonstrating anything about humans.
** Avoid using the potentially ambiguous term doctor to refer specifically to physicians or surgeons.
Avoid using doctor or physician in ways that incorrectly exclude other licensed healthcare professionals, such as nurses, physician assistants, and midwives.
Avoid using allopathic to describe modern Western medicine; instead use conventional medicine or mainstream medicine.

Avoid saying that people "suffer" from or are "victims" of a chronic illness or symptom, which may imply helplessness:  identifiers like survivor, affected person or individual with are alternative wordings.
Do not oversimplify the causes of suicide.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Music samples
rules.label: Manual of Style/Music samples
sections: What is the maximum length permitted?
key_statements:
== Guidelines ==

* Copyrighted, unlicensed music samples must be short in comparison to the original song.
As a rule of thumb, samples should not exceed 30 seconds or 10% of the length of the original song, whichever is shorter.
* Samples must be of reduced quality from the original.
ogg → mp3) has been performed by MediaWiki software, especially for web browsers and devices that do not support an original format.
For copyrighted music samples it should be .
* Each copyrighted music sample must be accompanied by a suitable fair use rationale, or it will be deleted.
* There should be only one sample per song recording, even if several users produce samples.
If a new sample is uploaded, the old one must be deleted.

===Inclusion in article===

*Contextual significance
:Properly uploaded music samples should only be added to articles in which the song or a particular aspect of it is discussed and referenced.
When using , the template should be placed in the paragraph where the song is discussed if possible.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Images/Sandbox
rules.label: Manual of Style/Images/Sandbox
sections: Do not use too many images
Do not repurpose images beyond their legitimate scope
Do not illustrate or introduce unpublished ideas
Remember accessibility for the visually impaired
key_statements: Images may be purely decorative in the technical sense that they convey no additional useful information and nothing happens when you click on them; but purely decorative images should still have a encyclopedic purpose in providing layout cues outside of article prose.

Avoid adding images that provide neither additional information (what the image looks like itself is not additional information unless the image is the subject of the article) to the article subject nor navigational or layout cues that aid the reader.
Images should serve a purpose other than solely decoration.

=====Do not use too many images=====
When images are added excessively, they clutter the page and become redundant.

=====Do not repurpose images beyond their legitimate scope=====
Images can represent a specific entity and should not be repurposed to represent something else, e.g.
For example, do not abuse the flag of the United Nations to represent the entire world, as this is not an accurate application of the official flag of that international organization.

=====Do not distort images=====
Do not modify or use non-generic images in a way that is not notably used outside of Wikipedia.
Image maps should specify alt text for the main image and for each clickable area; see Image maps and  for examples.
