* Physical anthropology terms such as "Caucasoid", "Negroid", and "Mongoloid" are not appropriate for contemporary ethnic, national or social identities, and should be used only in their correct scientific or historical context.
* "Chinese" or "Chinese person" are preferred over "Chinaman", which is outdated and has racist connotations; .

* "Roma" or "Romani" is preferred over "Gypsy" when referring to the ethnic group, although the terms are not always synonymous (especially when "gypsy" is lower-case and used to refer to itinerant people of unclear ethnicity).
Even Roma/Romani may not always be correct (see, e.g., Lom people, Dom people).

===Indigenous peoples===

* A 1996 survey revealed that a majority of persons from indigenous populations in the United States preferred "American Indian" to "Native American," although the latter is also generally acceptable.<ref>[http://www.infoplease.com/ipa/A0762158.html Preference for Racial or Ethnic Terminology]</ref>  Neither of these terms is generally used in Alaska, where the term Eskimo is sometimes offensive, and Alaska Native is usually preferred.
* In Canada, the term "Aboriginal peoples" is preferred over "natives" as a collective term.
The terms should only be used on Wikipedia in the narrow context of the United States.
* It is always preferable to refer to groups and individuals as specifically as is appropriate in the context.

===Gender and sexual identities===

* For people, the terms "gay" (often used for males only) and "lesbian" (which is used for females only) are preferred over "homosexual", which has clinical associations and may even be considered pejorative.
donts: * Do not assume that a different term is more inclusive<!--Intentional link to disambiguation page section; we mean everything in that section.--> or accurate.

Note that neutral terminology is not necessarily the most common term — a term that the person or their cultural group does not accept for themselves is not neutral even if it remains the most widely used term among outsiders.
* However, do not be so general as to render terms meaningless.

-->

===Avoid gratuitous references===

Do not call more attention to a person's identity labels than is strictly necessary.

* While it is often appropriate for an article to note that a person is notability, and to discuss the ways in which those identities have impacted their work, it is never necessary or appropriate for an article to constantly qualify the person as a "gay writer" or an "African-American linguist".

For example, it is usually more appropriate for an article to begin "John Smith is an American writer", and then discuss his sexual orientation later in the article, than for the article to begin "John Smith is a gay writer from the United States." Similarly, it is almost never necessary for the very first sentence of an article to point out that the subject is female.

===Pay attention to the person's specific cultural context===

Do not impose a label that you think should be used by the cultural group in question, if it's not the label that is used by the group in question.

* While "Colored" is now considered inappropriate as an racial label in the United States, "Coloured" it is the normal designation of a specific cultural group in South Africa which uses that term for itself.
Do not impose an alternate term that would be considered more acceptable in the United States but is not known or recognized by these South Africans.
Also, "black" should never be replaced with "African American" if the subject is not American.
Afro-Brazilian, Afro-Cuban) outside the US, but is disfavored in that country; avoid "Afro-American".

==Preferred terms==

===Ethnic and national identities===

* Avoid outdated terms when referring to present-day people.
* Avoid unclarified use of ambiguous terms.

"Russian" should not be used as a stand-in for "Soviet" when referring to a person from the era of the USSR whose ethnic identity is not known more specifically.<!--Need to also address "Indic" to refer to the cultures of the Indian subcontinent; Bangladeshis, Pakistanis, etc., do not like being called "Indians".
"Caucasian" should not be used to mean "white" in the ethnic/racial sense, except in quoted material, due the ambiguity and its disuse in this sense outside the US.
Avoid "Gypsy" when a more specific name in available.
"Indian" and "Eskimo" are not generally considered acceptable. Note that it is never appropriate to describe an aboriginal Canadian as "American Indian" or "Native American".
The specific culture to which a person belongs (e.g., Maya, etc.) should be used  of broad umbrella terms such as "Indian", "native" or "First Nations" whenever possible.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Organizing disambiguation pages by subject area
rules.policy_code: Organizing disambiguation pages by subject area
rules.label: Manual of Style/Organizing disambiguation pages by subject area
key_statements:
Clearly defined subject areas ===

The title of each section must make it clear what kinds of entries it contains, and  entries that fall within that subject area  be there.

If overlap can't  be avoided:
*:* For  items that fit in multiple sections: duplicate these entries in each appropriate section.

Moderate section size===

To minimize the reading users need to do on average, avoid both long, undivided sections, and lots of small sections:
* Sections with  should usually be divided into subsections, if practical.
* Sections with  should often (for only one entry, almost ) be combined, typically in an "Other uses" section.
If all the "other" entries in a section can be described by a plural noun, the words "uses in" should be avoided, e.g., , not ; , not .

(This is the easiest order to recognize – importance, for example, is usually too subjective.)

==Schemes to avoid==
Organizing schemes  based on subject area can be confusing.
Avoid organizing entries by etymology, pronunciation, or whether the terms are acronyms or not – these schemes assume knowledge that the reader may not have.
They should be liberally modified, supplemented, and promoted or demoted to different header levels to best suit each particular page.

without having to descend into irrelevant sections, and .

The title of each section must make it clear what kinds of entries it contains, and  entries that fall within that subject area  be there.
* Sections with  should often (for only one entry, almost ) be combined, typically in an "Other uses" section.

* At most  primary topic, if there is one, at the top of the page; and

Moderate section size===

To minimize the reading users need to do on average, avoid both long, undivided sections, and lots of small sections:
* Sections with  should usually be divided into subsections, if practical.
If all the "other" entries in a section can be described by a plural noun, the words "uses in" should be avoided, e.g., , not ; , not .

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility/Colors
rules.label: Manual of Style/Accessibility/Colors
key_statements: If a dark background is desired, it should be used against white text (#FFFFFF) without links or references.
dos:
For the civility essay dealing with colors, see Wikipedia:Don't edit war over the color of templates.}}<!--  -->


These color tables should help editors choose accessible backgrounds matching various color schemes.

Use the [https://webaim.org/resources/linkcontrastchecker/?fcolor=202122&bcolor=FFFFFF&lcolor=3366CC WCAG link contrast checker] to ensure that the chosen background color offers the recommended WCAG AA level of contrast against normal text (#202122) and blue links (#3366CC for the default Vector 2022 skin).<ref></ref> WCAG AA is required by various government bodies in the US, EU, UK and Canada.
If a dark background is desired, it should be used against white text (#FFFFFF) without links or references.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Biography/Survey on Style-Prefixed Honorary Titles/Ratification
rules.label: Manual of Style/Biography/Survey on Style-Prefixed Honorary Titles/Ratification
sections: A Note on Acting Before Ratification
Comments on Whig and Lulu of the Lotus-Eaters
Why current wording is problematic for some
Issues we should avoid going forward
key_statements: The question presented was whether the status quo ante represented a neutral point of view (NPOV) and/or whether it should be changed to a convention which refers to the formal style of address without using it at the start of the article.
Biographical entries in the Wikipedia should not begin with a prefixed style of formal address.

The prevailing alternative was prescriptive, and states:
:The formal style of address should always be provided in the introductory paragraph of the article, but only after the name is provided, and not otherwise prefixed.

If this ratification is unsuccessful, the Wikipedia:Manual of Style (biographies) will not provide a guide as to whether, how and where formal styles of address should be provided in biographical entries, until and unless a subsequent consensus on some convention can be reached.
All responses must be signed by valid users (Please use: <nowiki>~~~~</nowiki>) to be counted.
53% preferred this option the most, but that does not mean 47% disapprove of this policy.
Though it was my second choice, I think this should be the way we do things, as it seems to have the most consensus; mentioning of the style, without excluding it.

== REJECT Ratification ==
The formal style of address should always be provided in the introductory paragraph of the article, but only after the name is provided, and not otherwise prefixed. — REJECTED by those voting under this heading:

# No consensus; keep status quo.

Evil Monkey∴Hello 04:56, May 15, 2005 (UTC)
#: No, which is why THIS exists; 53% of users preferred this the most, but that does not mean 47% of people disapprove of it as being the official Wikipedia policy.
Maybe people who preferred option 1 on the survey over option 3 would consent to this, because they don't think it is bad.
Just because 53% preferred this over option 1 doesn't mean that 75% won't prefer this option to ambiguity.
Just because alternative 3 (what is being voted on) was not the most preferred option for everyone doesn't mean that it isn't an acceptable comprimise.
This is to determine whether a 3:1 ratio of users believe this should be the policy of Wikipedia.

--Vamp:Willow 16:48, 15 May 2005 (UTC)
#Strong Reject On the Wikipedia, 53% is not a consensus, and never will be.
Just because 47% of users preferred alternative 1 to this alternative does not mean all of them believe that this is not an acceptable Wikipedia policy.
What that 53% thing was was preferring this option to another option; it was preferred to the status quo (anarchy) by a significantly larger margin.
What we are trying to determine here is whether 3 out of 4 Wikipedia users think this should be Wikipedia policy.
After reviewing the discussion and the way it is being interpretted - that a 53% vote is a repudiation of the existing policy and that it should be overturned.
If you support this as Wikipedia policy, vote for this; if you do not, vote against it.
Vote for or against on the basis of whether this should be policy, and solely on that basis.

The prevailing alternative was prescriptive, and states:
:The formal style of address should always be provided in the introductory paragraph of the article, but only after the name is provided, and not otherwise prefixed.

If this ratification is unsuccessful, the Wikipedia:Manual of Style (biographies) will not provide a guide as to whether, how and where formal styles of address should be provided in biographical entries, until and unless a subsequent consensus on some convention can be reached.
All responses must be signed by valid users (Please use: <nowiki>~~~~</nowiki>) to be counted.
53% preferred this option the most, but that does not mean 47% disapprove of this policy.

older≠wiser 13:26, May 15, 2005 (UTC) #violet/riga (t) 16:14, 15 May 2005 (UTC) #Flyers13 16:42, 15 May 2005 (UTC) # Titanium Dragon 22:12, 18 May 2005 (UTC) I accept this vote; and this is about whether or not this should be the policy.
Though it was my second choice, I think this should be the way we do things, as it seems to have the most consensus; mentioning of the style, without excluding it.

== REJECT Ratification ==
The formal style of address should always be provided in the introductory paragraph of the article, but only after the name is provided, and not otherwise prefixed. — REJECTED by those voting under this heading:

# No consensus; keep status quo.

Evil Monkey∴Hello 04:56, May 15, 2005 (UTC)
#: No, which is why THIS exists; 53% of users preferred this the most, but that does not mean 47% of people disapprove of it as being the official Wikipedia policy.
Maybe people who preferred option 1 on the survey over option 3 would consent to this, because they don't think it is bad.
Just because 53% preferred this over option 1 doesn't mean that 75% won't prefer this option to ambiguity.
Just because alternative 3 (what is being voted on) was not the most preferred option for everyone doesn't mean that it isn't an acceptable comprimise.

(And Whig should be blocked for disruption for what he's doing supposedly on the basis of this survey.) Proteus (Talk) 10:15, 15 May 2005 (UTC)
#: Actually, it would simply mean that the style would not be the absolute law for the biography pages, as outlined above.
This is to determine whether a 3:1 ratio of users believe this should be the policy of Wikipedia.
Just because 47% of users preferred alternative 1 to this alternative does not mean all of them believe that this is not an acceptable Wikipedia policy.
donts: Biographical entries in the Wikipedia should not begin with a prefixed style of formal address.

== ACCEPT Ratification ==
The formal style of address should always be provided in the introductory paragraph of the article, but only after the name is provided, and not otherwise prefixed. — ACCEPTED by those voting under this heading:

# I accept this as a compromise consensus, with the understanding that the Wikipedia is often inconsistent, and if all pages do not conform to this convention, it will not violate manner of address is to be described in a neutral way, without being employed directly or endorsed by the Wikipedia.

--Vamp:Willow 16:48, 15 May 2005 (UTC)
#Strong Reject On the Wikipedia, 53% is not a consensus, and never will be.
If you support this as Wikipedia policy, vote for this; if you do not, vote against it.
If you do, vote yes, if you think this should not be the official Wikipedia policy, vote no.
This would indicate that they do not strongly oppose it, yes?
The convention being approved or rejected is as to how styles may be referred to, but prefixed styles should not be used.

I do not think this is what Jtdirl wants, but a desire to obstruct consensus seems to be guiding a number of people, and I think it serves the community poorly if we cannot find a way to constructively work together.

Biographical entries in the Wikipedia should not begin with a prefixed style of formal address. (emphasis in the original) A majority vote does not defeat an existing wikipedia policy - that is why this should be voted down.
Thus, it was never Wikipedia policy, unless you'd think that me changing the page unilaterally makes it Wikipedia policy.
I've never heard of it being done in such a manner, and I know that many people were distinctly uncomfortable with the voting system used, a voting system foreign to almost all of us.
Please do not accuse me of dishonesty or vandalism.
Never before the end of the survey was it ever billed as a matter of confidence or supply concerning the current policy.
Whig has no evidence whatsoever that a majority of people who do not think styles should be prefixing names at the beginning of articles feel this way because they think that styles are POV.
Because it is obvious you do not like being called on being disruptive, changing the biography template page unilaterally, ect.

When part of a chord progression, chords should be spaced equally if they take an equal portion of the progression's duration.

Generic titles should not be italicized.

True titles are always italicized:

When true titles are mixed with generic titles, as is often the case in suites, only the true title is italicized.
The generic portion of the title is not italicized and should always be in English even if the true portion of the title is in another language.
These should be in italics as well.

Any of these methods may be used; however, usage should be consistent within an article.
In this case, the tempo markings should be separated by en dashes set off by spaces (consider using the  template), and the first letter of each tempo marking should be capitalized.
For modern works, the key or index number may not exist, but the genre or performing force should always be specified.
However, in an article about a single composition of classical music, all the information one would get from a formal title should be included in the lead.
The en dash rather than the hyphen must be used for ranges of numbers (measures 1–4 rather than measures 1-4).

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Wikipedia books
rules.label: Manual of Style/Wikipedia books
key_statements:
Those found on user pages (User:USERNAME/Books/Title) can be written according to whatever style the user chooses.


==Scope and structure==
;Scope
Each book should have a specific and well-defined scope.
A reader should not have to guess what the book is about, or be surprised by its content.
However longer books, such as Book:Canada, will almost always benefit from a solid chapter structure.

==Naming==



Books should follow the same naming guidelines as articles do.
:*Parentheses should be removed from article titles when possible.
For example, Duality (mathematics) should be renamed to Duality unless confusion is likely to happen.
All of these should be written as they usually would be in articles.

== template==
This template should always be present at the beginning of books.
The title and subtitle parameters should match the title and subtitle of the book (level 2 and 3 headers, respectively).
An image should be chosen for the cover of the book, and the color is customizable.
Books should also reflect consensus.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Stringed instrument tunings
rules.label: Manual of Style/Stringed instrument tunings
key_statements:
{{nutshell|When describing the tuning of a stringed instrument:
* Always list the closest (normally bass) string first.
* Always number the furthest (normally treble) string as "one".
The notes should be named by uppercase letters, and separated by endashes.
Although the expert will instantly recognise either of these for what it is, for the benefit of the general reader a link should be provided to the Wikipedia article on whichever notation is used.
Either notation is equally acceptable, but within a single article one should be chosen and then used consistently, similarly to American versus British English.

In the case of coursed instruments such as the twelve-string guitar, courses should be separated by dashes, and string notes adjacent, so the twelve-string guitar tuned to octave G tuning is eE–aA–d′d–g′g–bb–e′e′ in Helmholtz notation.
Non-standard notations that may be confused with either Helmholtz or scientific pitch notation should be avoided.
All strings are numbered, so for example the string closest to the player of a twelve-string guitar is always string twelve, and never "string six secondary" or similar constructions.

Courses should be numbered if necessary in the same way as individual strings, starting with the course furthest from the player, thus a twelve-string guitar has courses numbered from one to six, while a nine-string baroque guitar has courses numbered from one to five.

===String gauges===

String gauges are always listed in the order in which the strings are conventionally numbered, rather than the order in which the pitches are conventionally listed, so for example a typical light set for electric guitar would be .009-.011-.016-.024w-.032w-.042w.
If in doubt, the recommended orientation is to have the string number as the leftmost column, and the first string as the top row.
This avoids having either order across the page, and so is consistent with either convention for listing pitches in music notation, and with listing either string pitches or gauges conventionally.
The recommended format works acceptably in most cases.
The recommended format copes well with these cases, and with combining what would otherwise be several tables into one.
Within a single article, either inch measure or thou measure should be used consistently.
In either case the unit may be omitted, and again either way this should be consistent throughout the article.

If all strings mentioned in an article are wound, for example on a bass guitar, the w suffix may be omitted so long as this is consistently done throughout the article, but this is not generally recommended.
If any string gauges within the article include plain (unwound) strings, then the w suffix should be shown for all wound strings.
An issue then arises, should the strings be listed in the order in which they are conventionally listed in the text, or in the order in which they conventionally numbered?
* One order or the other should be consistently followed within any one article.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Accessibility/Data tables tutorial
rules.label: Manual of Style/Accessibility/Data tables tutorial
sections: Avoiding column headers in the middle of the table
key_statements: , the  collapse control has to be inside a table header (until the collapsibility script is improved), and it must be large enough to contain it.

Death
| Episode: "Crossing the Line"
|}

Captions should be concise; if the table needs an extended introduction, provide one in normal article prose, then provide a simpler caption.
To clearly define relationships and avoid accessibility issues, use <code>!
scope="row" colspan="2" | row header 3 | data || data |} For tables with headers that are more complex, it is recommended to simplify the table or split it up into smaller tables.

Again, it is recommended to simplify the table so headers are visually clear by splitting it into a "d" and "e" table if possible where the text in the "d" and "e" cells are moved to each table's caption.

=== Images ===
* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: unknown (needs more testing and feedback for a precise rating)

Images inside a table should meet the general requirements in Wikipedia:Alternative text for images.

=== Color ===
* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: medium (needs testing and feedback for a precise rating)

Colors inside a table should meet the requirements for color.
But more importantly, information should not be conveyed by color alone.
Information should also be available textually.
A footnote or a textual sign<ref group="note">But avoid Unicode characters, per Wikipedia:Manual of Style (accessibility)#Text.
Nesting data tables with header cells also makes it difficult for assistive readers to parse them sensibly, and should be avoided.
Note that each table must begin on a new line.
It is unnecessary and is not recommended.
dos: The priority levels are determined by the Accessibility Success Criteria rankings A, AA, and AAA (in descending order of importance as accessibility considerations) of the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.0.<ref group="WCAG" name="WCAG2.0"> Confusingly, the WCAG 2.0's rankings A, AA (or Double-A), and AAA (Triple-A) are used for two different but interrelated concepts, the second of which may be counter-intuitive:<ol type="A"><li>The one used in this Wikipedia guideline – the relative importance of a particular "Success Criterion" at achieving accessibility, in which A is the most essential or impactful, and AAA represents less urgent accessibility allowances a site should make, with AA being of medium urgency.

Each criterion is explained at a "How to Meet" link in the section in WCAG 2.0 for each of its accessibility recommendations, and collected at [http://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG20/quickref/ "How to Meet WCAG 2.0: A customizable quick reference"]
  • The compliance level of a website, with "A" representing the minimum level of conformance to the accessibility recommendations, and "AAA" being the most accessible, meeting all Level A, AA, and AAA Success Criteria.

Wikipedia naturally strives for Level AAA Compliance, prioritizing on proceeding from A to AA to AAA compliance to meet the most essential accessibility requirements the soonest, where practical. The present system replaces the 1999 [http://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG10/ WCAG 1.0] system of Conformance Levels (also A, AA, and AAA) with a [http://www.w3.org/TR/WAI-WEBCONTENT/full-checklist.html checklist] of Priority 1, 2, and 3 recommendations; while those roughly correspond to the current A, AA, and AAA success levels, 2.0 has added many criteria that were not present in 1.0.

To prepare for the change we should use solely scope attribute on th.[http://www.w3.org/TR/2008/NOTE-WCAG20-TECHS-20081211/H63.html "H63: Using the scope attribute to associate header cells and data cells in data tables"], accessibility level: A === Layout of table headers === As can be seen in the example above, row headers are formatted by default as bold, centered and with a darker background.

=== Caption ===
* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: easy (the syntax is fairly simple and already in use; the layout slightly changes users' habits)

All data tables need a table caption that succinctly describes what the table is about.<ref name="H39" group="WCAG" /> It plays the role of a table heading, and is recommended as a best practice.<ref name="WebAccessibility 415"> GSA Schedule 70.

Table captions are made with |+.Table captions can also be made with }}, and summaries with "}}, but wiki syntax should be preferred in articles. A caption can be styled with CSS, and may include wikilinks, reference citations, etc.
, the  collapse control has to be inside a table header (until the collapsibility script is improved), and it must be large enough to contain it.

Death
| Episode: "Crossing the Line"
|}

Captions should be concise; if the table needs an extended introduction, provide one in normal article prose, then provide a simpler caption.
scope="row" colspan="2" | row header 3 | data || data |} For tables with headers that are more complex, it is recommended to simplify the table or split it up into smaller tables.

On the cells that are described by headers, set the "headers" to a spaced list of the describing "id" values in an order that a screen reader should read them.<ref name="H43" group="WCAG">[https://www.w3.org/TR/2008/NOTE-WCAG20-TECHS-20081211/H43.html "H43: Using id and headers attributes to associate data cells with header cells in data tables"], accessibility level: A</ref> For example, the following markup has the "Company" header describing the "ABC" company (<code>!

Again, it is recommended to simplify the table so headers are visually clear by splitting it into a "d" and "e" table if possible where the text in the "d" and "e" cells are moved to each table's caption.

=== Images ===
* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: unknown (needs more testing and feedback for a precise rating)

Images inside a table should meet the general requirements in Wikipedia:Alternative text for images.

=== Color ===
* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: medium (needs testing and feedback for a precise rating)

Colors inside a table should meet the requirements for color.
Information should also be available textually.
Note that each table must begin on a new line.
donts: The class plainrowheaders will apply left-aligned and normal-weight formatting so that editors do not feel the need to override the header formatting with inline CSS declarations for each cell.See the discussions at MediaWiki talk:Common.css [//en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=MediaWiki_talk:Common.css&diff=prev&oldid=395578619#some_wikitable_ideas some wikitable ideas] and [//en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=MediaWiki_talk:Common.css&diff=prev&oldid=395578619#Bold_row_headers bold row headers]. Used by itself, plainrowheaders will make headers appear similar to unmodified data cells, except for the darker background.

== Avoiding column headers in the middle of the table ==

* Priority: high (accessibility level: A)
* Difficulty: medium (requires large changes to tables, editors seem reluctant to split tables, needs more testing and feedback)

Do not place column headers in the middle of a table to visually separate the table.
To clearly define relationships and avoid accessibility issues, use <code>!
But more importantly, information should not be conveyed by color alone.
A footnote or a textual sign<ref group="note">But avoid Unicode characters, per Wikipedia:Manual of Style (accessibility)#Text.
Nesting data tables with header cells also makes it difficult for assistive readers to parse them sensibly, and should be avoided.

==Infobox==

First of all, everything in the highlights section of the infobox should be cited somewhere in the body of the article.

University of Florida Athletic Hall of Fame, Kansas Sports Hall of Fame)
*Do not include a section link to a highlights and awards section of the article, e.g.

*Hall of fame inductions do not need to be stated in the "highlights" section if they're already displayed with banners, e.g.

==References==


Everything in an article of a living person must be cited to reliable sources.
Self-published sources such as Twitter, Facebook, YouTube, or personal blogs should not be used in biographies of living persons unless published by that person.

==External links==

Navboxes should be grouped together when there are more than three.
Current roster navboxes and Template:Authority control do not count towards the total.

==Infobox==

===Highlights===
First of all, everything in the highlights section of the infobox should be cited somewhere in the body of the article.

Awards and highlights should be listed in the following order:<ref>Template talk:Infobox NFL biography/Archive 17#Championships listed first</ref>
# Pro championships
# Pro championship MVPs
# League MVPs
# Other major individual awards (Offensive Player of the Year, Defensive Rookie of the Year, etc.)
# First-team All-Pros
# Second-team All-Pros
# Pro Bowls
# Season statistical leaders (NFL passing yards leader, etc.)
# Pro career honors (retired number, etc.)
# College national championships
# College awards
# All-Americas
# Conference honors (SEC Defensive Player of the Year, etc.)
# All-Conferences (college only)
# College career honors
Highlights in the same category should be in chronological order.

==Body==


===Outline===
*An article for an NFL player should generally be outlined as follows:
*#Early life
*#College career
*#Professional career
*#Career statistics
*##NFL
*##College
*#Career highlights (if applicable; for NFL and college)
*##Awards and honors
*##Records
*#Personal life

===General notes===
* In most instances, and especially in prose, acronyms of football terms should be spelled out.

==References==


Everything in an article of a living person must be cited to reliable sources.

==External links==


==Navboxes==

Navboxes should be grouped together when there are more than three.

rules.title: Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Medicine-related articles/RFC on lead guideline for medicine-related articles
rules.policy_code: RFC on lead guideline for medicine-related articles
rules.label: Manual of Style/Medicine-related articles/RFC on lead guideline for medicine-related articles
key_statements: Should WP:MEDLEAD be in sync with WP:LEAD?
Please avoid threaded responses in the "Survey" section, and start a new sub-section in the "Discussion" section for threaded commentary.
Some of the forked guideline text directly contradicts Wikipedia's broader guidelines, and application of these recommendations has resulted in choppy prose, a loss of clarity, and over-cited leads.
Guidelines on making technical articles accessible are already available (MOS:JARGON and WP:TECHNICAL), and they do not include restrictions on sentence length.
Application of this sentence length restriction and oversimplified language has led to a loss of clarity and the precision required in medicine (see Talk:Schizophrenia example cited above).
Text that must be cited is clearly discussed in broader guidelines: no evidence that medical content is any different has been presented.
:Medicine project guidelines must remain consistent with Wikipedia's broader guidelines.

If the Wikipedia community wants to force citations and short sentences in a set order into leads for purposes of translation to other languages, that should be accomplished at WP:LEAD, not within the guidelines of one Wikiproject.

It said only:  "Adding sources to the lead is a reasonable practice but not required as long as the text in question is supported in the body of the article", which was in sync with Wikipedia policy and guideline.

Remove entire sentence.
: While the goals of translation are laudable, translation is not unique to medicine, and if the broader community wants en.Wikipedia lead guidelines to be rewritten to accommodate translation of leads only, that should be addressed at WP:LEAD.

Separate programs or products being advanced by [https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Wiki_Project_Med/Projects Wikimedia Foundation projects with their own governing boards] should not be constraining content in the English-language Wikipedia.

Requiring citations—beyond what en.Wikipedia policy requires, for the purposes of a Wikimedia project—should be examined at the level of Wikipedia-wide guidelines, and not in one WikiProject guideline.

2.

MOS:LEADCITE says:
:: {{talkquote|Because the lead will usually repeat information that is in the body, editors should balance the desire to avoid redundant citations in the lead with the desire to aid readers in locating sources for challengeable material.
The necessity for citations in a lead should be determined on a case-by-case basis by editorial consensus.
The lead of an article, , should be written as simply as possible .
: There is no such language anywhere in Wikipedia's lead guidelines, and no reason to expect medical articles should have a specific order of information in the lead.
Avoid cluttering the very beginning of the article with pronunciations or unusual alternative names; infoboxes are useful for storing this data.
However, this is not the website to argue that the rules must be followed without exception.
Guidelines should reflect best practice as performed by multiple editors in consensus, and must never be amended in order to support an idiosyncratic approach.
That shouldn't be how Wikipedia works.
dos: Should WP:MEDLEAD be in sync with WP:LEAD?

SandyGeorgia (Talk) 23:08, 30 December 2019 (UTC) * No, not in sync, but should be. Remove all content from the section, WP:MEDMOS#Lead: there is nothing unique about leads in medicine-related articles, this is a fork of a standard guideline, and there is little in this section that is in agreement with Wikipedia's broader guideline, WP:LEAD.
Some of the forked guideline text directly contradicts Wikipedia's broader guidelines, and application of these recommendations has resulted in choppy prose, a loss of clarity, and over-cited leads.
Application of this sentence length restriction and oversimplified language has led to a loss of clarity and the precision required in medicine (see Talk:Schizophrenia example cited above).
Text that must be cited is clearly discussed in broader guidelines: no evidence that medical content is any different has been presented.
:Medicine project guidelines must remain consistent with Wikipedia's broader guidelines.

If the Wikipedia community wants to force citations and short sentences in a set order into leads for purposes of translation to other languages, that should be accomplished at WP:LEAD, not within the guidelines of one Wikiproject.

It said only:  "Adding sources to the lead is a reasonable practice but not required as long as the text in question is supported in the body of the article", which was in sync with Wikipedia policy and guideline.

Remove entire sentence.
: While the goals of translation are laudable, translation is not unique to medicine, and if the broader community wants en.Wikipedia lead guidelines to be rewritten to accommodate translation of leads only, that should be addressed at WP:LEAD.

Requiring citations—beyond what en.Wikipedia policy requires, for the purposes of a Wikimedia project—should be examined at the level of Wikipedia-wide guidelines, and not in one WikiProject guideline.

2.
The necessity for citations in a lead should be determined on a case-by-case basis by editorial consensus.
The lead of an article, , should be written as simply as possible .

The effort should still be made to make the article as understandable to as many as possible, .}}
:: The blue highlighted wording is not problematic per se, rather its application requires fluency in the article's topic and literature that is not always evidenced by those advocating for this addition to MEDLEAD.
: There is no such language anywhere in Wikipedia's lead guidelines, and no reason to expect medical articles should have a specific order of information in the lead.
However, this is not the website to argue that the rules must be followed without exception.
donts: Please avoid threaded responses in the "Survey" section, and start a new sub-section in the "Discussion" section for threaded commentary.
Guidelines on making technical articles accessible are already available (MOS:JARGON and WP:TECHNICAL), and they do not include restrictions on sentence length.

Separate programs or products being advanced by [https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Wiki_Project_Med/Projects Wikimedia Foundation projects with their own governing boards] should not be constraining content in the English-language Wikipedia.

MOS:LEADCITE says:
:: {{talkquote|Because the lead will usually repeat information that is in the body, editors should balance the desire to avoid redundant citations in the lead with the desire to aid readers in locating sources for challengeable material.

The presence of citations in the introduction is neither required in every article nor prohibited in any article.}}
: To those who wonder why not fully cite medical leads anyway because extra citations can't hurt and may help: overcitation can and does impact readability and accessibility in leads by constraining the lead writer's ability to easily summarize general information well cited in the body.
Avoid cluttering the very beginning of the article with pronunciations or unusual alternative names; infoboxes are useful for storing this data.
Guidelines should reflect best practice as performed by multiple editors in consensus, and must never be amended in order to support an idiosyncratic approach.
"First, do no harm" trumps "first, do not violate MOS".

:The only point that actually goes against other guidelines (not simply extending upon them) is SandyGeorgia's point 6 — which states that pronunciation and rare alternate names should not be included in the lede body.
James even states on his user page that he may have some difficulty with spelling or grammar, though I do not know if this affects how he sees appropriate sentence length.

I regret that I failed to separate the issues at the outset, and apologize for the length because of my faulty framing; framing an RFC is never easy, and I regret that the first responders to this RFC declined to point out to me specifically why it was malformed.
(1) I've written my fair share of LEADS and have never felt constrained by the need to reference.
The "Me, too, per editor so-and-so" declarations that have become commonplace at WPMED have never led us anywhere useful, and dialogue is finally happening, so I am hopeful.
On your point (2), I believe the idea that we need citations in the lead to help avoid quackery and commercial interest content being inserted into leads is a strawman: Featured article review.

Whenever I do come back to look, I find deterioration (again, see the sample discussion at [https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Talk:Schizophrenia&oldid=932020654#FA_issues Schizophrenia]; we can't expect to fix them all).

More significantly, whether we look at B-class or GA/FA level, WPMED in general has moved away from systematically improving content in the body of articles (as once was done in the now-defunct Wikipedia:WikiProject Medicine/Collaboration of the Month/History), to a focus on more exclusively editing leads, to support off-en.Wikipedia projects.

Writers are not required to follow any of the following guidelines.
The joy of wiki editing is that perfection is not required.
In any case the title should appear in the first sentence.
Do not put links in the title.
*Avoid overuse of sub-headings.
When used generically, they should be in lower case: "De Gaulle was the French president." The correct formal name of an office is treated as a proper noun.
Likewise, royal titles should be capitalised: "Her Majesty" or "His Highness".
Pronouns referring to deities, or nouns (other than names) referring to any material or abstract representation of any deity, human or otherwise, do not begin with a capital letter.

===Calendar items===
The names of months, days, and holidays always begin with a capital letter: June, Monday, Fourth of July.
As a matter of truce both styles  are acceptable (except for proper names), but a redirect should be created from the alternative form.
Directions (north, southwest, etc.) are not proper nouns and do not start with a capital letter.
Indented quotations do not need to be marked by quotation marks.

If necessary to represent such characters as letters in article titles, the normal straight apostrophe ( ' ) should usually be used in place of the right quotation mark and the grave accent ( ` ) in place of the left quotation mark.

* In articles about International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) for chemical names wherever possible, except in article titles, where the common name should be used if different, followed by mention of the IUPAC name.

==Sections==
Main article: Wikipedia:Section

===Introduction===
The title or subject can almost always be made part of the first sentence, but some articles simply have names.
The appropriate lead length depends on the length of the article, but should be no longer than three paragraphs in any case.

==="See also" and "Related topics" sections===
Mostly, topics related to an article should be included within the text of the article as free links.
When the See also refers to the entire article, not just a section, it should be a heading of level 2 so that it appears in the table of contents.
*If a word/phrase that is used in one form of standard English is not generally understood by speakers of another form, either avoid it or explain it.
*Do not change the form of standard English adopted by an article without good reason.

Writers are not required to follow any of the following guidelines.
The joy of wiki editing is that perfection is not required.
In any case the title should appear in the first sentence.
When used generically, they should be in lower case: "De Gaulle was the French president." The correct formal name of an office is treated as a proper noun.
Likewise, royal titles should be capitalised: "Her Majesty" or "His Highness".

Again, when being used generically, no capital letter is used: "There are many prime ministers around the world." When reference is made to a specific office, upper case is generally used: "The British Prime Minister, Tony Blair, said today..." (However to complicate matters, some style manuals, while saying  "The British Prime Minister", recommend "British prime minister".

===Calendar items===
The names of months, days, and holidays always begin with a capital letter: June, Monday, Fourth of July.
As a matter of truce both styles  are acceptable (except for proper names), but a redirect should be created from the alternative form.

Example: :This is italic. which produces
This is italic.
===Titles=== Main article: Wikipedia:Manual of Style (titles) Italics should be used for titles of the following: *Books *Films *Long poems/epic poems *Musical albums *Periodicals (newspapers, journals, and magazines) *Plays *Television series *Works of visual art *Court cases *Ships *Computer games *Orchestral works *Named passenger trains.

Titles of shorter works, such as the following, should be enclosed in double quotation marks:
*Articles, essays or papers
*Chapters of a longer work
*Episodes of a television series
*Short poems
*Short stories
*Songs

There are a few cases in which the title should be in neither italics nor quotation marks:
*Scripture
*Legal or constitutional documents

===Words as words===
Use italics when writing about words as words.

If necessary to represent such characters as letters in article titles, the normal straight apostrophe ( ' ) should usually be used in place of the right quotation mark and the grave accent ( ` ) in place of the left quotation mark.

* In articles about International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) for chemical names wherever possible, except in article titles, where the common name should be used if different, followed by mention of the IUPAC name.

==Sections==
Main article: Wikipedia:Section

===Introduction===
The title or subject can almost always be made part of the first sentence, but some articles simply have names.

*The Pythagorean theorem is named for and attributed to the 6th century BC Greek philosopher and mathematician Pythagoras :The Pythagorean theorem is named for and attributed to the 6th century BC Greek philosopher and mathematician Pythagoras *Tom and Jerry — Pairing of names from Pierce Egan's Life in London :Tom and Jerry — Pairing of names from Pierce Egan's Life in London If the subject of the article has more than one name, each new form of the name should be in bold on its first appearance.
The appropriate lead length depends on the length of the article, but should be no longer than three paragraphs in any case.
donts: Do not put links in the title.
*Avoid links within headings.
*Avoid overuse of sub-headings.
Pronouns referring to deities, or nouns (other than names) referring to any material or abstract representation of any deity, human or otherwise, do not begin with a capital letter.

Philosophies, doctrines, and systems of economic thought do not begin with a capital letter, unless the name is derived from a proper noun:  Lowercase republican refers to a system of political thought; uppercase Republican refers to a specific Republican Party (each party name being a proper noun).

However, they do not start with a capital letter when they are used generally: "This summer was very hot."

===Animals, plants and other organisms===
Main articles:  Wikipedia:WikiProject_Tree_of_Life#Article_titles_and_common_names, Wikipedia:Naming conventions (fauna)

Whether the common names of species should start with a capital letter has been hotly debated in the past and has remained unresolved.
Directions (north, southwest, etc.) are not proper nouns and do not start with a capital letter.
Indented quotations do not need to be marked by quotation marks.

====Use straight quotation marks and apostrophes====
For uniformity and to avoid complications use straight quotation marks and apostrophes ( <big><big>' "</big></big> ) not curved (smart) ones, grave accents or backticks ( <big><big>‘ ’ “ ” `</big></big> ).

The characters may also be used in discussions about the quotation marks themselves.<!-- Get rid of the rest of the paragraph once the English Wikipedia supports Unicode throughout --> If using a left or right quotation mark for such a purpose, to ensure proper display on all browsers, do not type or paste such a quotation mark directly into the Wikipedia editor.
*If a word/phrase that is used in one form of standard English is not generally understood by speakers of another form, either avoid it or explain it.
*Do not change the form of standard English adopted by an article without good reason.

Nevertheless, here are some guidelines:
*The first priority is to use terminology that will be understood or readily accepted by the reader.
*Do not assume that any one term is the most inclusive or accurate.

===Don't get fancy===
It is easier for you and whoever follows you if you do not try to get too fancy with your markup.
